text: You can only submit one application for each theme.
The value of each application must be between £1,000 and £25,000.
To be eligible to apply for a grant, your business must be based and registered in England.
To be eligible to apply for a grant, you must be a farmer, horticulturalist, forestry owner, or contractor carrying out services to farmers, horticulturalists or forestry owners.

The Animal Health and Welfare grant is only available if you farm beef cattle, dairy cattle, sheep, pigs, laying chickens (including rearing and breeding farms), or broiler chickens (including rearing and breeding farms).
You must follow these regulations if you are a land manager in England.
You must comply with these regulations if you are in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ).
You must take steps to prevent agricultural diffuse pollution occurring.

You must identify and take into account any factors that could lead to the risk of pollution, including the angle of slopes (in particular if greater than 12 degrees), amount of ground cover, proximity to inland freshwaters, coastal waters, wetlands, springs, wells or boreholes, soil type and condition, and the presence and condition of land drains.
You must plan each application of organic manure or manufactured fertiliser on your agricultural land.
When planning applications of organic manure or manufactured fertiliser, you must check that you do not apply more than your soil or crops need at the time of application.
When planning applications of organic manure or manufactured fertiliser, you must assess the pollution risks so you do not create a significant risk of pollution.
When planning applications of organic manure or manufactured fertiliser, you must take into account the weather forecasts and conditions at the time you want to apply it.
If you are applying organic manure or manufactured fertiliser to cultivated agricultural land, you must take into account results of soil tests in your plans.
Soil test results must show the pH and levels of nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium (with the option of an assessment of soil nitrogen supply instead of a soil test for nitrogen).
Soil test results must be no more than 5 years old at the time of application.
You must not apply manufactured fertiliser on waterlogged, flooded or snow covered soil.
You must not apply manufactured fertiliser when the soil has been frozen for more than 12 hours in the past 24 hours.
You must not apply manufactured fertiliser within 2 metres of inland freshwaters, coastal waters, a spring, well or borehole.
You must not apply manufactured fertiliser where risk factors mean there is a significant risk of pollution.
You must not apply organic manure on waterlogged, flooded or snow covered soil.
You must not apply organic manure when the soil has been frozen for more than 12 hours in the past 24 hours.
You must not apply organic manure within 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.

You must not apply organic manure within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters, unless you are using precision spreading equipment or you are managing land for specific environmental benefits.
You must not apply organic manure where risk factors mean there is a significant risk of pollution.

Where the exception for environmental benefits applies (land managed for breeding wader birds or as species-rich semi-natural grassland), you may only apply livestock manure (not slurry or poultry) within 10 metres of inland freshwaters and coastal waters if it is a requirement of an agri-environment scheme, the land is within a site of special scientific interest, you do not apply manure onto the surface of water, you only apply the manure from 1 June to 31 October, and you apply no more than 12.5 tonnes per hectare per year.
You must take reasonable precautions to reduce the risk of pollution when you apply organic manure or manufactured fertiliser.
You must take into account risk factors for run-off when deciding where to store manure on your land.
You must not store manure within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters.
You must not store manure within 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.
You must not store manure anywhere there is a significant risk of pollution.
You must comply with the relevant regulations for storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil if you store silage or slurry on your land.
You must comply with the relevant regulations for storing organic manures in nitrate vulnerable zones if your land is in an NVZ.
You must take reasonable precautions to prevent soil erosion caused by livestock, land management or cultivation practices.

You must take reasonable precautions to reduce the risk of pollution when you carry out activities such as creating farm tracks or gateways, establishing seedbeds, polytunnels or tramlines, cleaning out ditches, installing drainage or irrigation, irrigating crops, and spraying crops with pesticides, herbicides or fungicides.
You must make sure you prevent livestock compacting soil by trampling it (poaching) within 5 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters.
You must not place livestock feeders within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters.
You must not place livestock feeders within 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.
You must not place livestock feeders where risk factors mean there is a significant risk of pollution.
You must take reasonable precautions to prevent pollution when managing livestock.
You must store slurry safely to avoid pollution from animals, manure and silage.
You must make sure you meet the rules if you are a farmer, land manager, or tenant farmer (unless you can prove someone else, for example the leaseholder, is jointly or wholly responsible).
A new or substantially changed store must follow the specific rules for the substance you are storing.

A new or substantially changed store must have an expected lifespan of at least 20 years with maintenance; any part of a silage effluent tank that is underground must last 20 years without maintenance.
A new or substantially changed store must be at least 10 metres clear of inland or coastal waters (you may need a larger safety zone in some cases, for example near a water supply intake).
You must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before you begin construction of a new or substantially changed store.
You must make sure you do not pollute the surrounding environment (for example rivers or other watercourses) when building or modifying a store.
You must check if you also need planning permission before starting construction or substantial changes to a store.
Once your storage is built, you must carry out maintenance to make sure there is no risk of pollution.
You must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before you build new storage for silage, slurry or agricultural fuel oil, or make substantial changes to an existing store.
You must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before you store field silage on a new site.
You must not make or store any silage, or unwrap baled silage, within 10 metres of inland or coastal waters.
You must not store field silage within 50 metres of a protected water supply source (any place where water is abstracted for human consumption, use in farm dairies, or human food preparation).
All parts of a silo must be resistant to attack.
Your silo must have an impermeable base extending beyond any walls.
Your silo must have impermeable drainage collection channels around the outside, flowing into an effluent tank.
The silo base must comply with British Standard 8007:1987 and British Standard 8110-1:1997 (for concrete bases) or British Standard 594/EN 13108-4:2006 (for hot-rolled asphalt bases).
If your silo has walls, they must withstand wall loadings set in British Standard 5502-22:2003+A1:2013.
Your silo must have an effluent collection system.

If all parts of an effluent tank are above ground, the tank must be constructed to resist attack from silage effluent for at least 20 years with maintenance. If any part is below ground, it must be impermeable for at least 20 years without maintenance.
You must never put silage effluent into an under-floor slurry store.

You must make sure you have enough effluent tank capacity to avoid the risk of pollution (at minimum, the capacity rules must give at least 2 days' storage at peak flow: 20 litres per cubic metre for silos up to 1,500 cubic metres; 30 cubic metres plus 6.7 litres per cubic metre of capacity over 1,500 cubic metres for larger silos).
You must seal baled silage in an impermeable membrane or bag.
You must make sure any baled silage stored directly on the ground cannot leak into a water source.
You must keep baled silage at least 10 metres clear of inland or coastal waters.
For field silage, there must be no construction works (for example walls or earth banks) and topsoil must not be disturbed.
If you make field silage or non-baled bags, you must choose a suitable site (for example, if you store silage in a non-baled bag in your farmyard, you must make sure you can contain any drainage).
If you make field silage or non-baled bags, you must notify your local Environment Agency office at least 14 days before you first use the site.
You must collect and store slurry (run-off from solid manure stores, woodchip, straw-bedded corrals, and stand-off pads) either separately or in your main slurry store.
You must not put slurry storage tanks, effluent tanks, channels or reception pits within 10 metres of inland or coastal waters without written agreement from the Environment Agency.

Slurry tanks, reception pits, pipes and channels must be impermeable and meet the anti-corrosion standards set in British Standard 5502-50:1993+A2:2010, and should last for at least 20 years with maintenance.
The base and walls of your slurry tank and any reception pit must withstand the wall loadings set in British Standard 5502-50:1993+A2:2010.

When calculating the required capacity of a steel or concrete slurry store, you must include a minimum of 300 millimetres of freeboard; for earth-banked stores you must include a minimum of 750 millimetres of freeboard and maintain this during use.
You will normally need slurry storage capacity for at least 4 months.
Any temporary slurry storage facilities (such as a reception pit) must be large enough to store a minimum of 2 days' combined slurry production and likely rainfall.

If your slurry tank, effluent tank or reception pit is fitted with a drainage pipe, it must have 2 valves separated by at least 1 metre, each valve must be able to shut off the flow of slurry, and each valve must be locked when not in use.

An earth-banked slurry store must have impermeable soil to a thickness of at least 1 metre (with permeability of less than 1 x 10⁻⁹ metres per second, tested to BS 1377-6:1990 or BS EN 1997-2:2007); alternatively, you may import impermeable soil or use a synthetic liner.
The base of a weeping-wall store must extend beyond its walls and include perimeter drains to catch escaped slurry and drain into an effluent tank.
Your slurry irrigation system must not deposit slurry within 10 metres of inland freshwater or 50 metres of any water supply.
You must assess the risk of direct run-off from land you intend to irrigate with slurry, and identify unsuitable areas.
Agricultural fuel oil must be stored in a tank or drum that meets ISO 9000.
No part of an agricultural fuel oil installation can be within 10 metres of inland or coastal waters, including yard drains, dry ditches and land drains.
If agricultural fuel oil tanks are underground, they must also follow the Defra groundwater protection code.
A tank or drum storing agricultural fuel oil must be surrounded by a secondary containment structure or bund.
The bund for agricultural fuel oil storage must have a life expectancy of at least 20 years with maintenance.
The bund must be impermeable to oil and water (walls and base), with no drain-down pipe.
The bund must contain every part of the tank, with taps and valves directed down and locked shut when not in use.
The bund must contain delivery pipes that are permanently attached to the primary tank; those pipes must be fitted with self-closing taps or valves and locked inside the bund when not in use.
You must dispose of any waste fuel oil or mix of oil and water that collects in the bund without causing pollution.

The bund capacity must be at least 110% of the capacity of a single tank; for multiple tanks, at least 110% of the largest tank or 25% of the total volume of all tanks in the area, whichever is greater; for other storage such as drums, at least 25% of the total volume that could be stored at any time.
If you are served a notice requiring improvements to your store, you must carry out the required work within at least 28 days (or any longer period allowed).
If you wish to appeal against a notice, you must do so in writing to the Secretary of State within 28 days from the day after the notice was served, and send a copy to your Environment Agency office.

Your appeal letter must explain why you are appealing and should include a copy of the notice, any related correspondence, and a plan of your farm showing the installation and any watercourses or drains.
If you are acting for a farmer who was served the notice, you must include a statement from them confirming this in your appeal.

When calculating nutrients for the soil, you should calculate the nutrient levels that will be supplied from organic sources, such as legumes, manure, slurry or digestate, or nutrients recycled from the return of crop residues (for example, straw).
You must not spread waste that does not benefit the land.
You must not spread more waste than you need to benefit the land — any excess is classed as disposal.
You must not spread waste on non-agricultural land.
The waste you store and spread must fit within the waste codes and descriptions in the table.
You must not store or spread more than the maximum amount shown in the tables for each type of waste.
The site where you store and spread the waste (except for dredging spoil) must be at least 10 metres from a watercourse.
The site where you store and spread the waste (except for dredging spoil) must be at least 50 metres from a spring, well or borehole.
You must store waste in a secure location, ensuring that waste cannot escape and the public cannot gain access to it.
You can only store U10 waste types for a maximum of 12 months.
Milk may only be stored for a maximum of 24 hours — you must use it within 24 hours of storing.
Biobed and biofilter material must be stored for at least 12 months before you can use it.
You must not spread waste on land that is waterlogged, frozen or covered with snow.
You must not spread waste on land that has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the 24 hours before you start spreading.
Dredging spoil must be spread on land next to where it was dredged.
Waste milk must be diluted with an equal or greater amount of water or slurry before it is spread.
Waste milk must be spread on the same area no more than once every 4 weeks.
Waste milk must not be stored for longer than 24 hours.
Sludges, wash waters and effluents from washing fruit or vegetables on a farm can only be spread on land at the same farm.
Ash (waste code 10 01 01) must have been produced under U4 exemption: burning waste as fuel in a small appliance, before it can be used under a U10 exemption.
Biobed and biofilter material (waste code 19 08 12) must have been produced under T32 exemption: treating waste in a biobed or biofilter, before it can be used under a U10 exemption.
Biobed and biofilter material must be stored for at least 12 months before it is spread.

Compost (waste code 19 05 99) must have been produced under a T23 exemption, T26 exemption, or a permit that only allows waste types listed in T23 and T26 exemptions, before it can be used under a U10 exemption.
If compost comes from a permitted site, the facility must ensure the compost is stable, sanitised and suitable for agriculture.
The permitted site supplying compost must be allowed to accept only the exact waste types listed in T23 or T26.
Compost produced from waste types listed in T23 exemption must have been made according to strict mixing ratios: for every 60 tonnes of compost there must be no more than 10 tonnes of cardboard.
Compost produced from waste types listed in T23 exemption must have been made according to strict mixing ratios: for every 60 tonnes of compost there must be no more than 20 tonnes of manure.

Compost produced from waste types listed in T23 exemption must have been made according to strict mixing ratios: for every 60 tonnes of compost there must be no more than 10 tonnes of any of the following: animal tissue waste (02 02 02), materials unsuitable for consumption or processing (02 05 01, 02 06 01), biodegradable kitchen and canteen waste (20 01 08), or biodegradable waste from markets only (20 03 02).

Digestate (waste code 19 06 04) must have been treated under a T24 exemption, T25 exemption, or a permit that only allows waste types listed in T24 and T25 exemptions, before it can be used under a U10 exemption.
If digestate has been treated under a permit, the conditions of the permit must state that the waste is stored in the digester for at least 28 days before going to the U10-registered site.
You cannot use digestate from sites with a permit that allows other waste types.

You must keep a chronological record of waste activity carried out under this exemption, recording the date of activity, the amount of waste spread, the type of waste spread, and where the waste came from.
You must keep your records for 2 years and make them available to the Environment Agency when asked.
You cannot have more than one of the same exemption at a site at the same time.
You cannot register an exemption if it has already been registered at the site by another business.
You cannot renew your registration more than one month before the 3-year registration period ends.
You cannot increase the storage limit by registering a separate storage exemption.
You must not store waste that is not included in your exemption.
If a U10 exemption is deregistered before it expires, any new U10 registration at the same site inherits the remaining waste limit left by the previous registration.
You must comply with the terms and conditions of the exemption.
You may only store waste under this exemption for up to 12 months.
You may only store waste that you produced yourself.
You must store the waste at the place where you produced it.
The waste must be stored securely, meaning it cannot escape from its container and the public cannot access it.
Any treatments carried out on the waste must not change the characteristics of the waste.
You cannot store waste that was produced somewhere else.
You cannot store waste for longer than 12 months.

You must apply for a bespoke environmental permit to rear pigs or poultry intensively in an installation if you have more than 40,000 places for poultry, 2,000 places for production pigs (over 30kg), or 750 places for sows.
You must not operate without a permit when you are required to have one.
You must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying for a new intensive farming permit.
You must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying to change (vary) an existing permit that could affect ammonia emissions.
You must submit your permit application within 6 months of your ammonia screening assessment to make sure the data remains current.
You must produce a written management system — a set of procedures that identifies and minimises the risks of pollution.

You must check that you meet the legal operator and competent operator requirements, including your technical competence, your financial status, and any previous convictions for environmental offences.
You must do an environmental risk assessment to check the potential effects of your installation on air, water, land and your neighbours.
You must design your installation to control and monitor emissions to air, water and land.
You must meet best available techniques (BAT).

If you are applying for a new permit, or varying your permit, you must meet the BAT conclusions for the intensive rearing of poultry or pigs where the application relates to new sites with new livestock housing, or existing sites that are expanding where the variation includes both existing and new livestock housing.
You must download and fill in form part B3.5 – new bespoke intensive farm installation when applying for your intensive farming permit.
When you send your completed application form you must also include all the documents listed in the application checklist in section 15 of form part B3.5.
You must pay an application fee when you send your application.
If your application is successful, you must pay the Environment Agency an annual subsistence fee.
When you get your permit, you must keep to the conditions in your permit.
When you get your permit, you must meet BAT standards.
You must follow this guidance if a part or all of the farm buildings from which your slurry comes is within an NVZ.
You must also follow the guidance on the rules for using nitrogen fertilisers in NVZs and storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil.
If you separate slurry you need to store and spread the separated liquid and more solid organic manure correctly.

You must be able to store all the slurry you produce and all poultry manure produced in a yard or building during the storage period, unless you reduce the volume by sending it off your farm or spreading some of it on fields with a low risk of runoff.
The storage period runs from 1 October to 1 April inclusive (6 months) for pigs and poultry.
The storage period runs from 1 October to 1 March inclusive (5 months) for cattle, sheep, goats, deer and horses.
In addition to storing the slurry you produce, you must provide storage for any slurry, rainfall, washings or other liquid that enters the store during the storage period.
When you calculate the slurry storage capacity you'll need, you have to allow for wetter than average rainfall years (1 in 5 year).

If you spread slurry or poultry manure on fields with a low risk of runoff to reduce the amount of storage capacity you need to provide, you must still comply with the closed period for spreading slurry.

If you spread slurry or poultry manure on fields with a low risk of runoff, you'll also need to take account of the limits on spreading slurry or poultry manure from the end of the closed period until the end of February.
Land used as low risk of runoff must have an average slope of less than 3 degrees.
Land used as low risk of runoff must not have land drains (other than a sealed pipe).
Land used as low risk of runoff must be at least 50 metres from a watercourse or conduit leading to a watercourse (like a ditch or working land drain).
You must provide storage facilities for an additional 1 week's manure as a contingency measure in case you cannot spread on fields with a low risk of runoff on some days.
For new or substantially changed slurry storage you also need to comply with the slurry storage rules.

You must store poultry manure, other types of solid organic manure or animal bedding that contains organic manure in one of the following ways: in a container; on a waterproof base, where you can collect and store runoff; in a roofed building; or in a temporary field heap.
You must make sure your field heap is at least 10 metres from any surface water (such as a river, pond or ditch) or land drain, or 30 metres if the land slopes at 12 degrees or more.
You must make sure the location of the field heap is not liable to being waterlogged or flooded.
You must locate field heaps at least 50 metres from a spring well or borehole.
You must move the field heap at least every 12 months.
You must leave a 2 year gap before returning to the same field heap site.
You must keep a record of the sites used for field heaps and the dates of use.
You must show all the low risk areas suitable for temporary field heaps on your risk map if you plan to use them.
Temporary field heaps must be made from manure solid enough to be stacked in a freestanding heap.
Temporary field heaps must not give rise to free drainage from within the stacked material.
Temporary field heaps must be covered with a waterproof material if they contain poultry manure without bedding or litter.
Temporary field heaps must occupy as small a surface area as is needed to support the weight of the heap without it collapsing.

If you're constructing new storage for slurry, reconstructing part of a storage facility or enlarging your current storage facilities, you must follow the rules on storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil.
You must record the capacity of the storage facilities on your farm and update any changes to this within 1 week of the change.
You must record your calculation of the volume of manure that will be produced by the livestock you'll keep in a building or on hard standing during the storage period.
You must record your calculation showing the amount of storage you need.
You must record any changes in volume and storage capacity due to introducing livestock onto your farm for the first time within 1 month of the change.
You must record your calculations to show how you've reduced your volume by sending slurry off your farm or spreading it on low-risk areas (if relevant).
By 30 April each year you must make a record of the numbers and type of livestock you kept in buildings or on hard standing during the previous storage period.
By 30 April each year you must make a record of the dates for the start and end of use and locations of any field sites you use for storing solid manure.
You must keep these records for 5 years.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 2 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
To be eligible for the grant, you must farm pigs, beef or dairy cattle.
To be eligible for the grant, your farming system must already produce slurry.
Invited applicants must have submitted their slurry store location and design assessment form by 11.59pm on 30 September 2024.
Once your project has passed the location and design check, you must submit your full application by the deadline date in your invitation email.
The grant must be used to replace, build additional, expand or cover existing slurry stores in order to reach the grant storage requirements of 6 months for beef and dairy cattle.
The grant must be used to replace, build additional, expand or cover existing slurry stores in order to reach the grant storage requirements of 8 months for pigs.
You must notify the Environment Agency at least 14 days before you construct new storage for silage, slurry or agricultural fuel oil, or make substantial changes to an existing store.
You must know the address of the store before notifying the Environment Agency.
You must provide your contact details when notifying the Environment Agency.
You must confirm the store is not within 10 metres of coastal waters or inland freshwaters.
You must confirm the store is not within 50 metres of a spring, well or bore-hole.
Your activity must meet the rules set out in the permit.
You must be able to manage the potential risks as set out in the generic risk assessment.

You cannot store waste within a zone defined by a 50 day travel time for groundwater to reach a groundwater abstraction that is used to supply water for homes or producing food (this is known as a Source Protection Zone 1).
You cannot store waste under this exemption where it is to be used at another place.
You cannot store waste where it is not going to be used in accordance with the Sludge (Use in Agriculture) Regulations 1989.
You cannot store waste within 0.3 metres of the top of an open storage container or within 0.75 metres of the top of an earthbank tank or lagoon.
You must ensure your waste fits within the relevant waste code and description as listed in the List of Wastes (LoW) Regulations.
You may store no more than 1,250 tonnes of waste at any one time.
You may store waste for no more than 12 months before using it.
The waste must be stored at least 10 metres from any watercourse.
The waste must be stored at least 50 metres from any spring or well, or from any borehole not used to supply water for homes or producing food.
The waste must be stored at least 250 metres from any borehole used to supply water for homes or producing food.
The waste must be stored in a secure location at the place where it is to be used.
You must use the sludge in accordance with the Sludge (Use in Agriculture) Regulations 1989.
You need to register this exemption with the Environment Agency if you will meet the requirements.
The operator must operate mobile plant only for land treatment activities on notified agricultural or non-agricultural land.
The operator must apply for a deployment each time they wish to spread the wastes allowed under their permit.
The operator must use the LPD1 deployment form and guidance when applying for a deployment.
You must use the specified ammonia emission factors when carrying out ammonia assessments as part of your intensive farming permit application.
You must use the specified ammonia emission factors when calculating your annual emissions for reporting to the pollution inventory.
For free range poultry, you must use the combined housing and ranging emission factors for pollution inventory reporting.
If you operate a housing system not listed in the guidance, you may need to provide evidence of a site-specific ammonia emission factor as part of your permit application.
You must follow the exemption conditions, including specific conditions and requirements for certain types of waste.
You cannot spread waste that does not benefit the land.
You cannot spread more waste than you need to benefit the land, as any excess is classed as disposal.
You cannot spread waste on agricultural land under this exemption.
The waste you store and spread must fit within the waste codes and descriptions given in the table.
You must not store or spread more than the maximum amount shown in the tables for each type of waste.
The site where you store and spread the waste (except for dredging spoil) must be at least 10 metres from a watercourse.
The site where you store and spread the waste (except for dredging spoil) must be at least 50 metres from a spring, well or borehole.
You must store waste in a secure location, ensuring waste cannot escape and the public cannot gain access to it.
You can store U11 waste types, except biobed and biofilter material, for up to 12 months.
Biobed and biofilter material must be stored for at least 12 months before you can spread it.
You must not spread waste on land that is waterlogged, frozen or covered with snow.
You must not spread waste on land that has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the 24 hours before you start spreading.
Dredging spoil must be spread on land next to where it was dredged.
Biobed and biofilter material must be stored for at least 12 months before being spread.
Ash (waste code 10 01 01) must have been produced under U4 exemption before it can be used under a U11 exemption.
Biobed and biofilter material must have been treated under T32 exemption before it can be used under a U11 exemption.
Biobed and biofilter material must be stored for at least 12 months before it is spread.
Compost must have been produced under T23 exemption, T26 exemption, or a permit that only allows waste types listed in T23 and T26 exemptions before it can be used under a U11 exemption.
If compost comes from a permitted site, the facility must ensure the compost is stable and sanitised and will improve the soil structure or nutrients in land.
If compost comes from a permitted site, the site must be allowed to accept only the exact waste types listed in T23 or T26.
Compost from waste types listed in T23 must have been made according to strict mixing ratios, meaning that for every 60 tonnes of compost there must be no more than 10 tonnes of cardboard.
Compost from waste types listed in T23 must have been made so that for every 60 tonnes of compost there is no more than 20 tonnes of manure.

Compost from waste types listed in T23 must have been made so that for every 60 tonnes of compost there is no more than 10 tonnes of any of the following waste types: animal tissue waste (02 02 02), materials unsuitable for consumption or processing (02 05 01; 02 06 01), biodegradable kitchen and canteen waste (20 01 08), or biodegradable waste from markets only (20 03 02).

Digestate must have been treated under T24 exemption, T25 exemption, or a permit that only allows treatment of waste types listed in T24 and T25 exemptions before it can be used under a U11 exemption.
If digestate has been treated under a permit, the conditions of the permit must state that the waste is stored in the digester for at least 28 days before going to the U11-registered site.
You cannot use digestate from a facility with a permit that allows other waste types.
You must keep a chronological record of waste activity carried out under this exemption.
You must record the date of activity, the amount of waste spread per hectare, the type of waste spread, and where the waste came from, in the order the activity took place.
You must keep your records for 2 years and make them available to the Environment Agency when asked.

You do not need to keep a record under this exemption if you already do so under the Nitrate Pollution Prevention Regulations 2015 or the Water Resources (Control of Agricultural Pollution) (Wales) Regulations 2021.
You cannot have more than one of the same exemption at a site at the same time.
You cannot register an exemption if it has already been registered at the site by another business.
You cannot renew your registration more than one month before the 3-year registration period ends.
You cannot increase the storage limit by registering a separate storage exemption.
You cannot store waste that is not included in your exemption.
Once a U11 exemption is registered, the maximum waste limit applies to the site for 3 years on a tonnes-per-hectare-per-year basis.
If the exemption is deregistered before it expires, any new U11 registration inherits the remaining waste limit that was left by the previous registration.
You must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying for a new intensive farming permit.
You must request an ammonia screening assessment from the Environment Agency before applying to change (vary) an existing permit that may affect ammonia emissions.
You must complete the relevant ammonia screening assessment data spreadsheet forms.
You must complete an online ammonia screening assessment request form.

Before requesting your ammonia screening assessment, you must check that you can comply with the requirements of the Best Available Techniques (BAT) Reference Document (BREF) for the Intensive Rearing of Poultry or Pigs (IRPP).
You must confirm in your proposals that all livestock housing meets BAT.
You must demonstrate the new housing (new plant) will meet the BAT-AELs for ammonia emissions and nitrogen and phosphorus excretion, where applicable.
You must review the 'BAT-AELs' sheet in the data spreadsheet forms for a comparison of current ammonia emission factors against the BAT-AELs.
You must submit information with your application to confirm the proposed housing systems will meet the relevant ammonia BAT-AELs.
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with a breakdown of livestock numbers by type and by housing.
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the ventilation system type (fan or natural).
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the ventilation location (roof or side mounted).
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the height of roof vents and the fan airflow speed in metres per second (if fans are roof mounted).
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the amount of manure stored on your farm at any one time, in tonnes.
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the surface area (in square metres) of slurry storage facilities on your site, including lagoons.
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the type of cover used on slurry storage (if present).
You must download and complete the relevant data spreadsheet forms with the most appropriate ammonia emission factors for the livestock and housing type.
You must save the completed spreadsheet form and upload it when you submit the online ammonia screening request form.
When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide the farm name, address and Ordnance Survey grid reference.

When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide a site plan (for new applications or variations that include changes to the site layout), which should include the locations of all livestock housing, manure and slurry storage and the installation boundary in relation to local features such as named roads or buildings.

When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide a description of your proposal (or proposed changes if you are varying your permit), including BAT conclusions compliance and any changes to livestock numbers and types, manure storage, installation infrastructure and the installation boundary.

When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide relevant site history, for example if an ammonia screening has been completed within the last 6 months, if you have a site of high public interest, or if there have been previous emissions issues associated with the farm.
When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide the ammonia screening assessment data spreadsheet form (or forms).
When requesting an ammonia screening assessment, you must provide a permit or pre-application reference number.
You must submit your intensive farming permit application within 6 months of your ammonia screening assessment to make sure the data remains current.
You must not dispose of the biogas by flaring except if appliances burning biogas break down or when they are being maintained.
You must not dispose of the biogas by venting it to the atmosphere.
You must not compost the manure and plant tissue under this exemption (if you want to do this, you need to register exemption T23 – Aerobic composting).
You must not spread the digestate produced under this exemption.
You must ensure the waste you treat fits within the relevant waste code and description as listed in the List of Wastes (LoW) Regulations.
You may store or treat up to 1,250 cubic metres of waste at any one time.
If you import manure and slurry from other farms and store it before it is fed into your AD plant, the storage of this waste must be counted within the 1,250 cubic metre limit.
When manure and slurry is mixed with plant tissue waste, the 1,250 cubic metre limit includes the storage of plant tissue waste, material in the digester and the storage of the resulting digestate.
You must keep the waste in the digester for at least 28 days.
You must collect and burn the biogas produced by the AD process to produce energy.
You must use a net rated thermal input of less than 0.4MW on the AD plant biogas burner.
If there is more than one burner associated with the AD plant, you must have a combined net rated thermal input of less than 0.4MW.

Where the only waste feedstock to the anaerobic digestion process is farmyard manure or slurry, and the digestate is spread on agricultural land as a fertiliser or soil conditioner, you must still comply with the code of good agricultural practice and any requirements for nitrate vulnerable zones.
You must register this exemption with the Environment Agency if you meet the requirements.
If you cannot comply with the limits and quantities in this exemption, you must apply for an environmental permit.
Spreading waste to land must be controlled by an environmental permit, regulatory position statement, low risk waste position or waste exemption.
Spreading waste to land must result in agricultural benefit or ecological improvement.
Spreading waste to land must be waste recovery, not waste disposal.
Your landspreading activity must not endanger human health.
Your landspreading activity must not harm the environment by causing risk to groundwater, surface water, air, soil, plants or animals.
Your landspreading activity must not cause nuisance through noise or odours.
Your landspreading activity must not adversely affect the countryside or places of special interest.
If you hold a mobile plant permit for landspreading you must apply for a deployment before you store or spread waste to land.
You must use form LPD1 application for deployment (LPD1) when applying for a deployment.
With your deployment application you must provide a benefit statement.
With your deployment application you must provide a location map.
With your deployment application you must provide the full analytical results for the waste and receiving soil.
Your benefit statement must provide evidence that your landspreading activities are a waste recovery activity.
Your benefit statement must provide evidence that your landspreading activities will result in agricultural benefit or ecological improvement.
You must not start any activities until you have received written agreement (an email decision notice) from the Environment Agency.
You must tell your local Environment Agency office before you start landspreading, at least 48 hours (but no more than 7 days) before the date you start.
If your spreading activities have to stop for more than 7 days, you must tell the Environment Agency when you will restart spreading.
If you need to continue waste operations after 12 months, you must apply for a new deployment.
Your activities must meet the requirements of your permit and your agreed LPD1.
You must not make any changes to your deployment without the Environment Agency's agreement.
If you are concerned about not being able to comply, you must contact your local Environment Agency office.
You must comply with an environmental permit for a waste operation or relevant waste exemption when you treat, store or spread sewage sludge and septic tank sludge to land.

You must meet the requirements set out in the RPS in order to benefit from the Environment Agency's position of not normally taking enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit or waste exemption requirement.
You must comply with all other legal requirements that apply to your activities, as the regulatory position does not apply to any legal requirements beyond those specifically addressed.
Manure should be stacked for a period of 8 weeks before spreading on land.
Once a manure stack is formed, no further manure should be added to the stack for the entire 8-week stacking period.
The manure stack should be sited so as to prevent livestock gaining access.
If possible, the manure stack should be sited on hard standing and covered once formed, to prevent access by wildlife (including birds).
Slurry should be held for a period of 3 months prior to spreading on land, where the farm has adequate storage capacity.
During the 3-month slurry holding period, no further slurry is to be added.
Solid manure should be stacked until it is well rotted before use on land, as fresh manure can harm some plants.
Slurry or manure should be spread on arable land rather than pasture which will be grazed or cropped for animal feed, to reduce transmission of bacteria from manure to other animals.

If manure or slurry is applied to grazing land or land cropped for feed production, grazing or cropping should not take place for a period of at least 8 weeks, in order to reduce the risk of spreading antibiotic resistant bacteria to livestock.
Slurry should be injected rather than top-spread, to reduce the spread of resistance by reducing runoff and minimising wildlife exposure.

Appropriate hygiene and bio-security precautions should be taken when spreading or handling manure, including wearing outer clothing and waterproof footwear which can be cleaned and disinfected, wearing gloves, hand washing, and ensuring that manure is not stored or treated anywhere near food crops which are eaten without cooking such as salads.
Manure must be transported in covered leak-proof containers in conditions which prevent unacceptable risks to animal and public health.
Additional requirements apply for farms or fields located in nitrogen vulnerable zones.
Stacked manure and slurry that has been batch-stored for a minimum period of 6 months prior to spreading may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling or planting.

Composted solid manure or lime-treated slurry, where the process includes a storage period allowing the compost or slurry to mature for at least 3 months prior to spreading, may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling or planting.
Solid manure and slurry which is untreated or which does not meet the specified treatment conditions must not be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops within 12 months of harvest.

Manure or slurry applied to land used to grow ready to eat vegetables should be incorporated into the soil as soon as is practicable, to reduce the potential for direct crop contamination and to reduce odour and ammonia emissions.

Digestate from slurry or manure processed in an anaerobic digestion plant that complies with BSI PAS 110 requirements may be applied to land used to grow ready to eat crops any time before drilling and planting.
If you hold an environmental permit for intensive pig and poultry farming, you must follow the relevant guidance to comply with the conditions in your permit.
You must apply no more than 170kg per hectare of nitrogen in livestock manure on your holding in a nitrate vulnerable zone (NVZ) in each calendar year, as an average across your holding.
You must make applications for a derogation on the basis of the Environment Agency's interactive map.
You cannot get permission to use more nitrogen from non-grazing livestock (veal calves, pigs or poultry) and must keep within the 170kg per hectare limit for such livestock.
If your derogation application is successful, you must comply with the conditions of the derogation and the NVZ rules.
You can only apply for a derogation if at least 80% of the agricultural area of your holding is grass.
You must apply between 10 November and 31 December 2025 for a grassland derogation in 2026.
When you apply, you must give the farm name, address and your County Parish Holding (CPH) number and provide a map showing the location and extent of the land parcels that make up your holding.
When you apply, you must give the agricultural area and grassland area of your farm in hectares on 1 January of the year for which you are applying.
When you apply, you must give the amount of manure nitrogen produced by grazing livestock kept on your farm during the calendar year in kilograms of nitrogen (kgN).
When you apply, you must give the amount of manure nitrogen produced by grazing livestock you intend to bring onto or send off your farm during the calendar year (kgN).
When you apply, you must give the total amount of manure nitrogen from grazing livestock on your farm during the calendar year (kgN).
When you apply, you must give the amount of manure nitrogen produced by non-grazing livestock on your farm during the calendar year (kgN).
When you apply, you must give the amount of manure nitrogen produced by non-grazing livestock you intend to bring onto or send off your farm during the calendar year (kgN).
When you apply, you must give the total amount of manure nitrogen from non-grazing livestock on your farm during the calendar year (kgN).
If your application is successful, you must formally agree to fulfil the conditions of your derogation in writing to the Environment Agency.
If your application is refused, you must follow the 170kg nitrogen per hectare limit.
You can appeal within 30 days of your application being refused.
You must meet all the conditions of your derogation and follow the rules that apply when using nitrogen fertilisers and storing organic manures in NVZs.

If the EA withdraws your derogation or decides you are ineligible to apply for a derogation the following year, you will not be allowed to apply more than 170kg of nitrogen per hectare in livestock manure, averaged across your holding.

If you plough grassland where you have a derogation, you must plant a crop with a high nitrogen demand (for example, grass, potatoes, sugar beet, maize) within 4 weeks from the date of ploughing, or grass within 6 weeks from the date of ploughing.
You must not plough temporary grassland on sandy soils between 1 July and 31 December.

You must not plough any area of grass before 16 January if you've spread livestock manure on that area in the previous year between 1 September and 31 December on sandy soils, or between 15 October and 15 January on all other soils.
You must not grow leguminous or other atmospheric nitrogen-fixing plants in your rotation, except grass with less than 50% clover or legumes under-sown with grass.
You must plan the amount of phosphate fertiliser you put on your land in an NVZ.

Before you use phosphate for the first time in a field where you're going to plant a crop or have planted a crop, you must calculate the amount of phosphate in the soil that's likely to be available for the crop to use during the growing season.

Before you use phosphate for the first time in a field where you're going to plant a crop or have planted a crop, you must calculate the amount of phosphate that should be applied to the crop to get the best crops, taking into account the phosphate already available in the soil.

Before you use phosphate for the first time in a field where you're going to plant a crop or have planted a crop, you must calculate the amount of phosphate from any planned applications of organic manure that's likely to be available to the crop in the growing season in which you spread it.
Before you use phosphate for the first time in a field where you're going to plant a crop or have planted a crop, you must calculate the amount of manufactured phosphate fertiliser you will need.
You must carry out steps 3 and 4 of the phosphate planning process every time you spread manure or fertiliser.

Before you use manure or fertiliser on any crop (including grass) in following growing seasons, you must repeat steps 1 and 2 of the phosphate planning process and plan your fertiliser use for that growing season.
For permanent grassland, you must make your phosphate calculations based on the farming year starting on 1 January.
You must sample and analyse the soil at least once every 4 years.
You must sample and analyse the soil for at least every 5 hectares of your holding that has the same cropping and soil type.

If you have not got sampling data that's less than 4 years old when your derogation starts, you'll need to sample 75% of your farm area in the first year of your derogation, and sample the remaining 25% of your farm area in the year of your next derogation.
In your fertilisation plan and field records you must record your nitrogen use and any phosphate you use from manufactured fertilisers and organic manures.
You must record how much phosphate and nitrogen any manure contains within one week of bringing it onto your holding.
If you do not know the phosphate content of manure you bring onto your farm, you must analyse or calculate the amount and record it within a week of finding it out.
By 1 March of each year in which you have a derogation, you must have recorded the size of the agricultural area of your holding in hectares, and update this within one month if it changes.
By 1 March of each year in which you have a derogation, you must have recorded the size of the area of grassland for that calendar year in hectares, and update this within one month if it changes.

By 1 March of each year in which you have a derogation, you must have recorded a written description of the livestock housing and manure storage systems you have on your farm, including the volume of the manure storage available.
By 1 March of each year in which you have a derogation, you must have recorded the numbers and type of livestock you expect to have on your holding during the remaining part of the calendar year.
By 1 March of each year in which you have a derogation, you must have recorded an estimate of the manure nitrogen and phosphate that your livestock will produce during the current calendar year.
You must send the EA details about your farming practices and fertiliser use for the period 1 January to 31 December by 30 April in the year following the derogation as your fertilisation account.

Your fertilisation account must show the total agricultural area of your derogated holding and the area (in hectares) covered by each of the specified crops: winter wheat, spring wheat, winter barley, spring barley, winter oilseed rape, sugar beet, potatoes, forage maize, and grass.
Your fertilisation account must show the area covered by other crops (for example, vegetables, lucerne, flowers), though the specific crop does not need to be named.
Your fertilisation plan must show the number and category of livestock kept on your farm, and the amount of nitrogen and phosphate in the manure they produce.
Your fertilisation plan must show the amount and type of livestock manure brought on or sent off your farm, together with the total amount of nitrogen and phosphate in that manure.
Your fertilisation plan must show the weight in tonnes and nitrogen content of all manufactured nitrogen fertiliser stocks kept on, brought in or sent off your farm during the calendar year.
You must apply to deploy mobile plant for landspreading waste.
You must provide the correct information at the right level of detail when making your application, using the specified guidance documents.
You must produce a benefit statement as part of your application.
You must provide a waste and receiving soil analysis as part of your application.
If you cannot apply online, you must apply using form LPD1.
You may need to obtain planning permission for waste operations, including some mobile plant deployments, for example for some types of storage such as lagoons.
You must meet certain processing requirements to sell manure, guano or digestive tract content as fertiliser in the EU.
If you do not meet EU processing standards, you may still sell your product as fertiliser by partially processing it using heat or pressure.
You must contact APHA to apply for approval to make fertiliser from partially processed manure or guano.

When processing digestive tract content into a prill for sale as fertiliser, you must comply with the restrictions that apply to prills made from digestive tract content as set out in the guide to fertilisers made from processed ABPs.
You need approval to store an organic fertiliser or soil improver if you receive a fertiliser made from manure, guano or digestive tract content in bulk and then repackage it for final sale.
You must contact APHA to seek approval to store a fertiliser made from manure, guano or digestive tract content that is received in bulk and repackaged for final sale.

When spreading non-poultry manure, guano or digestive tract content on your own land, you may need an Environment Agency waste disposal permit unless you qualify for an exemption from waste permitting.

It is good practice to avoid spreading manure, guano or digestive tract content on land where farmed animals have access, or to stop animals grazing on the land for 21 days (or 2 months in the case of pigs) after it has been spread.

To spread poultry manure on your own land or supply it for use on other farms and market gardens, you must inspect your birds regularly and remove any dead ones from the litter immediately so carcasses do not end up in the manure.
To spread poultry manure on your own land or supply it for use on other farms and market gardens, you must not dispose of feathers or cracked eggs in your manure.
If you spread poultry manure on land where farmed animals graze, you must stop animals grazing on the land for 21 days (or 2 months for pigs).
If you spread poultry manure on land where farmed animals graze, you must stack the manure as far as possible from the livestock.
If you spread poultry manure on land where farmed animals graze, you must fence in the manure until you use it.
You must get approval to burn poultry manure in a combustion unit.
You must complete and keep a self-assessment and annual vet review form to show the Red Tractor assessor and your vet when using recycled manure solids as cattle bedding.
You must contact the APHA Centre for International Trade in Carlisle if you want to import processed manure from a country outside the EU.
You cannot import unprocessed manure from countries outside the EU.

If you are storing manure, guano or digestive tract content anywhere other than where it was produced or where it is going to be used, the storage site must be approved to store category 2 ABP material.
To get approved to store ABP material at a site other than where the material was produced or will be used, you must complete form ABPR3 and send it to APHA.
If you move digestive tract content or guano off the premises where it was produced, you must follow the requirements for transporting ABP material.
If you move digestive tract content or guano off the premises where it was produced, you must keep commercial documents with the ABP material.

You must follow the transport and commercial document requirements when handling manure, except when moving manure between farms or when moving manure from a farm to the place where it is going to be used.
You do not need to label manure, guano or digestive tract content as ABP material.
Your activity must meet the rules set out in the permit.
You must be able to manage the potential risks as set out in the generic risk assessment.
You must get approval to burn poultry manure in a combustion unit on your farm or holding.
The poultry manure you burn must be unprocessed.
The poultry manure you burn must be from your own land.
If your unit produces less than 5 megawatts (MW), you must apply to APHA.
You must fill in the application and inspection form, which describes the rules you must follow.
The maker of your combustion unit must fill in the combustion unit declaration form to confirm the unit meets EU standards.

If you are in England, you must send both the application and inspection form and the combustion unit declaration form to the APHA Customer Service Centre - One Health, Worcestershire County Hall, Spetchley Road, Worcester, WR5 2NP (email: CSCOneHealthABP@apha.gsi.gov.uk).
If you are in Scotland or Wales, you must send both forms to your local APHA field service office.
If your unit produces more than 5MW and you are in England or Wales, you must contact the Environment Agency.
If your unit produces more than 5MW and you are in Scotland, you must contact the Scottish Environment Protection Agency.
Your activity must meet the rules set out in the permit.
You must be able to manage the potential risks as set out in the generic risk assessment.
You must pay a charge for this waste exemption (band 2).
You may only mix manure or slurry with ash from the incineration of pig and poultry carcasses.
You may only spread the manure or slurry ash mix on your own farm to benefit the soil.
You may store the ash before mixing it with the manure or slurry, and you may store the manure or slurry ash mix before spreading it.
You must not take ash from neighbouring farms and mix it with manure and slurry on your farm.
You must not mix any other ashes with manure and slurry and spread the mix on your land.
You must not plough ash straight into the soil.
You must ensure the waste you use fits within waste code 19 01 12 (ash from the incineration of pig and poultry carcasses only) and its description under the List of Wastes (LoW) Regulations.
You must not spread more than 150kg of ash per hectare in any 12-month period under this exemption.
You should only spread the amount of ash that the soil needs.
You must not store or spread the ash mix within 10 metres of a watercourse or 50 metres of a spring, well or borehole.
You must mix the ash with an equal or larger amount of manure or slurry before spreading it.
The ash must be stored in a secure place before it is spread.
You must not spread the ash mix on land that has been frozen for 12 hours or more in the preceding 24 hours.
You must not spread the ash mix on land that is frozen, waterlogged or covered by snow.
The pig and poultry carcasses must be incinerated in compliance with the Animal By-Product Regulations.
This exemption can only take place on the same farm where the pigs or poultry died and were then incinerated.
You must register this exemption with the Environment Agency if you meet the requirements.
You must meet all the conditions of a Low Risk Waste Position (LRWP) in order to carry out an activity without an environmental permit for a waste operation.
You must regularly check that the LRWP you are benefiting from is still in place.
Only invited applicants can apply for round 1 of the Slurry Infrastructure grant.
The grant must be used to improve or expand slurry storage capacity to 6 months based on existing livestock numbers.
To be eligible to apply, you must farm pigs, beef or dairy cattle.
To be eligible to apply, your farming system must already produce slurry.
The slurry store must be built on land you own or farm as a tenant.
You must submit your full application by 11:59pm on 28 June 2024.
You must complete your application and email it with any supporting documents to ftf@rpa.gov.uk.
You must apply for a deployment each time you wish to spread the wastes allowed under your permit.
You must use the LPD1 deployment form and guidance when applying for a deployment.
You must apply for a standard rules environmental permit in order to operate mobile plant for landspreading of sewage sludge at a specified location.

If you hold a landspreading permit (SR2010 No 4, SR2010 No 5, SR2010 No 6, or a bespoke mobile plant permit for landspreading or land reclamation), you must comply with the rules set out in your permit.
You must contact the Environment Agency if your slurry or milk store is at risk of overflowing or leaking.
You must contact the Environment Agency if you cannot avoid spreading slurry or milk on agricultural land and there is a risk of slurry runoff, run-through to land drains or leaching.
You must contact the Environment Agency if you are at risk of breaching the legal requirements of nitrate vulnerable zones.
You must contact the Environment Agency if you are at risk of breaching the legal requirements of storing silage, slurry and agricultural fuel oil (SSAFO) rules.
You must contact the Environment Agency if you are at risk of breaching the legal requirements of rules for farmers and land managers to prevent water pollution.
You can only spread milk to land if you have a U10 waste exemption.
You must have a contingency plan to avoid causing pollution during exceptional circumstances.
You must make sure that all your staff and contractors are aware of your contingency plan.
You must normally comply with SSAFO rules to store slurry.
You must only consider temporary storage where existing facilities are inadequate.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must check planning requirements with your local planning authority.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must contact the Environment Agency before construction.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must agree each individual location with the Environment Agency.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must install tanks, liners and slurry bags to manufacturer's instructions.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must make sure the base of earth bank lagoons is above the water table - there should be at least one metre of clay subsoil beneath the proposed base.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must use a trial pit to confirm the depth of the clay layer - the resulting hole must be backfilled and puddled in.

To keep slurry in a temporary store you must use liners where there is doubt about soil permeability - lower grade liners should suffice for temporary storage but use high grade liners in high risk areas.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must monitor it to make sure there are no leaks.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must locate it at least 10 metres from watercourses and land drains - use temporary trial trenches if you are unsure about the presence of land drains.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must locate it at least 50 metres from groundwater sources.
To keep slurry in a temporary store you must de-commission it as soon as it's no longer needed.
If you are using shared facilities you must consider any biosecurity risks.
If you are using shared facilities you must agree management arrangements.
If you are using shared facilities you must agree where responsibility lies.
You must spread slurry or milk thinly and widely.
You must spread slurry or milk at an application rate not exceeding 20 cubic metres per hectare - you must use a lower application rate if run-off could enter surface water.
You must only spread slurry or milk on land with low run-off risk.
When exceptional circumstances apply, you must contact the Environment Agency before you spread any slurry or milk to land.
When exceptional circumstances apply, you must agree with the Environment Agency that spreading is the only option available.
When exceptional circumstances apply, you must only spread the amount of slurry and milk you need to reduce the risk of pollution.
When exceptional circumstances apply, you must only spread up to 20 cubic metres per hectare and on the lowest risk land available.
When exceptional circumstances apply, you must carry out regular checks before, during and after spreading to ensure there is no pollution taking place.
You are still responsible for any pollution that you cause.
You must read the General information for imports of animal by-products - Import Information Note GEN/3.
You must read the Clarifying information for imports of animal products - IIN GEN/4.
Your activity must meet the rules set out in the permit.
You must be able to manage the potential risks as set out in the generic risk assessment.
You must notify the Animal and Plant Health Agency and follow certain conditions if you plan to use recycled manure solids (RMS) as bedding for dairy cattle.
If you use a weeping wall, you must store and manage both the liquid and solid materials as slurry.
If you use chemicals like coagulants with mechanical separation, you may need an environmental permit, and you must check with the Environment Agency if you are unsure.
You must follow conditions if you wash used sand-based animal bedding for reuse and spread wash waters for irrigation.
All farms that produce slurry and spread it on their land must have at least enough storage to hold all the slurry produced in any 4-month period and comply with Farming Rules for Water (FRfW).
If your farm is in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ), you need at least 5 months of slurry storage for cattle, sheep, goats, deer and horses.
If your farm is in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (NVZ), you need at least 6 months of slurry storage for pigs and poultry.
You must have enough slurry storage to meet the legal minimum storage requirements without relying on the separator, so that you have sufficient storage if the separator fails or needs maintenance.
You must collect your slurry in a reception pit before separation, and the pit must be large enough to hold all the slurry you are likely to produce in a two-day period, including any rainfall.
If you install a separator on a gantry, you must fit the gantry with an access ladder and platform to allow safe inspection.
After separation, the liquid must be transferred to a slurry store built to comply with the minimum capacity and build standards required by the SSAFO regulations.
You must collect and store the stackable material on an impermeable surface to prevent leaching into the soil.
If liquid leaks from stackable material, you must capture it and divert it to your slurry store.
If stackable material gets wet again, you must store and manage it as slurry.
You must not keep stackable material within 10 metres of inland freshwaters or coastal waters, including those that are seasonally dry.
You must not keep stackable material within 50 metres of any spring, well or borehole.
You cannot spread liquid with high readily available nitrogen during closed periods in Nitrate Vulnerable Zones, even if there is a soil and crop need.
If stackable material remains high in readily available nitrogen, you cannot spread it during closed periods in Nitrate Vulnerable Zones, even if there is a soil and crop need.
If you have an environmental permit to rear pigs and poultry intensively, you must monitor your livestock's annual nitrogen (N) emissions.
If you have an environmental permit to rear pigs and poultry intensively, you must monitor your livestock's annual phosphorus (P) emissions.
If you have an environmental permit to rear pigs and poultry intensively, you must report your livestock's annual nitrogen (N) emissions.
If you have an environmental permit to rear pigs and poultry intensively, you must report your livestock's annual phosphorus (P) emissions.
You must submit your annual emissions data by 28 February of each year.
Your N and P emissions data must cover the previous year, from 1 January to 31 December.
You must report N and P emissions for broilers.
You must report N and P emissions for laying hens.
You must report N and P emissions for turkeys.
You must report nitrogen emissions for ducks (nitrogen only, not phosphorus).
You must report N and P emissions for weaners (7kg to 30kg).
You must report N and P emissions for fattening pigs (over 30kg).
You must report N and P emissions for sows (including piglets).
You must report N and P data for each livestock category separately.
Your emissions must meet the best available techniques (BAT) associated emission levels for total N and P.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must complete one calculation for each rearing cycle.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must include the specific number of birds per cycle in your calculation.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must include the number of days in the cycle in your calculation.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must include the number of days between cycles in your calculation.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must add the results together to get your annual total.
Where you use varying stocking rates throughout the year, you must make sure the total days equals 365.
When collecting manure samples using Method 2, you must take at least 10 sub-samples from different places or depths.
When collecting manure samples using Method 2, you must make the sub-samples into one composite sample.
When collecting manure samples using Method 2, you must sample the bottom of litter for poultry.
When using Method 2, you must use your own site-specific data, not standard figures.
You must use the emissions reporting form to report the data to the Environment Agency.
You must complete the N and P emissions for each type of livestock you keep before you submit the form.
The operator named on the permit must capture, treat and store biogas from lagoons and tanks in accordance with the rules of the permit.
You must pay a fee of £2,641 when applying for a new standard rules permit.
You must pay a fee of £792 when applying for a minor variation to the permit.
You must pay a fee of £2,529 to transfer the permit.
You must pay a fee of £2,356 to surrender the permit.
You must pay an annual subsistence charge of £1,336 for the standard rules permit.
You must have an environmental permit where one is required.
You must register an activity as exempt where you are required to do so.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms must submit to inspection of all structures on the farm, including slurry or manure stores.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms must submit to inspection of all structures on the farm, including animal housing.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms must submit to inspection of all structures on the farm, including oil and chemical storage, feed storage, and carcass storage.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms must submit to inspection of all structures on the farm, including foot dips.
All permitted intensive pig and poultry farms must submit to inspection of all structures on the farm, including drainage systems.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection training records.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection a complaints procedure.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection records of energy use, raw materials, water use, waste and carcass disposal.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection records of livestock numbers.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection feed dockets.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection records of manure export and soil and manure nutrient analysis.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection an accident management plan.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection an inspection and maintenance plan.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection an odour management plan.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection a noise management plan.
Operators must maintain and make available for inspection a manure management plan.

If you are a member of the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme, you will be inspected once a year over a 3 year period, with the Environment Agency conducting one inspection and a certification body conducting the other 2.
If you are not in the Pig and Poultry Assurance Scheme, the frequency of inspection will depend on the risk of pollution from your farm.
Operators must comply with any actions set out in the Compliance Assessment Report sent by the Environment Agency following an inspection.
Operators who have serious problems meeting the standards will receive a follow-up visit from the Environment Agency.
Manure, guano or digestive tract content must be treated at an approved processing plant before being sold as fertiliser in the EU.
To get approval to process manure, guano or digestive tract content, you must contact the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA).
Manure, guano and digestive tract content must be heated to 70°C for at least 60 minutes before they can be sold in the EU as fertiliser.
If you use an alternative processing method, you must be able to show APHA that your method is effective at making the product safe to use.

You must write a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) to explain how you will control and record temperature processing conditions (e.g. temperature, presence of chemical agents, time spent in the cooker) during your treatment.
You must write a Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points plan (HACCP) to explain how you will manage hazards including the content of unprocessed raw material.
You must carry out a test to show that your alternative treatment is effective at controlling levels of bacteria, viruses and other parasites.
If you use a heat treatment, you must show your process controls the presence of bacteria like Enterococcus faecalis.
If you use a heat treatment, you must show your process controls infectivity titres of viruses like Parvovirus.
If your treatment involves chemicals, you must also show that it controls the presence of eggs of parasites like the Ascaris parasite.
Before your validation test, you must add indicators to your raw material that have a similar heat and chemical resistance to the organisms you need to control.
If you use a heat treatment, your process must reduce the presence of the indicator of Enterococcus faecalis by 5 log 10.
If you use a heat treatment, your process must reduce the presence of the indicator of parvovirus by 3 log 10.
If your treatment involves chemicals, your process must also reduce the presence of the indicator of eggs of the Ascaris parasite by 3 log 10.
You must take samples from each batch of your processed product to check if your treatment is effectively controlling bacteria levels.
You must send samples to a laboratory for tests for Salmonella and either E. coli or Enterococcaceae.
You must take samples for E. coli or Enterococcaceae testing immediately after you have finished treating the manure, guano or digestive tract content.
You must take samples for Salmonella testing when you remove your product from storage to send it away from your facility.
To prevent contamination of your processed product, you must store it in either well-sealed and insulated silos or storage sheds, or sealed packs such as plastic bags or 'big bags'.
You must contact the APHA Centre for International Trade in Carlisle if you would like to send your processed product to another EU state.
You must complete commercial documents for trading ABPs between EU states.
You must have an environmental permit for a waste operation when you use waste clay to construct slurry lagoons or irrigation/winter storage reservoirs.
To avoid enforcement action by the Environment Agency for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement, you must meet the requirements set out in the RPS.

You must comply with all other legal requirements that apply to your activity, as the regulatory position does not apply to any other legal requirements beyond the environmental permit requirement it addresses.
You must use this licence when you need to move used poultry litter, manure or slurry from a premises in a protection or surveillance zone.
You must use this licence when you need to spread the moved poultry litter, manure or slurry in a protection or surveillance zone.
You must only use this licence in England and Wales.
You must have an environmental permit for a waste operation when you store and use farmyard and horse manure to breed worms to make a wormery.
To avoid enforcement action by the Environment Agency for not holding an environmental permit, you must meet all the requirements set out in the RPS.

You must comply with all other legal requirements that apply to your activity, as the regulatory position only applies to the specific permit requirement and does not affect any other legal obligations.
You must have an environmental permit for a waste operation when you store and treat litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak.
You must comply with the conditions of your environmental permit when you store and treat litter, straw, slurry and washwater from an animal disease outbreak.
To benefit from the Environment Agency not normally taking enforcement action for non-compliance with the environmental permit requirement, you must meet the requirements set out in this RPS.
